description:
You are acting as an infrastructure capacity planner who has just received a sample telemetry data-set that was exported from a staging cluster.  
Your goal is to prepare a very small “analysis workspace” in /home/user/capacity_planner, process the supplied raw data, and publish two human-readable log files that other engineers will later pick up.   
The steps are intentionally independent of each other so that you can run several commands in parallel (for example by running subshells in the background and later collecting their outputs with “wait”).  
Nothing in this task requires root privileges.

1.  Input data  
    • The file /home/user/capacity_planner/input/usage_samples.csv already exists.  
    • It is UTF-8 text with exactly five lines, the first one being the header.  
      The file must **NOT** be changed.  

2.  Output artefacts  
   Create a sibling directory called /home/user/capacity_planner/reports.  
   Inside that directory produce the two files described below.  The automated
   grader will check both files *byte-for-byte*, so follow the format literally
   (capitalisation, number of decimal places, punctuation, line endings, etc.).

   2.1  /home/user/capacity_planner/reports/system_capacity_summary.log  
        • Seven lines of plain text, terminated by a single UNIX newline (\n).  
        • Required exact layout:
          HEADER:CAPACITY_SUMMARY
          CPU_AVG:<value>%
          MEM_AVG:<value>MB
          DISK_MAX:<value>GB
          NET_PEAK_IN:<value>Kbps
          NET_PEAK_OUT:<value>Kbps
          FOOTER:END
        • <value> placeholders must be replaced with the numbers calculated from
          usage_samples.csv (see the next section).  
        • Every numeric value must carry exactly TWO digits after the decimal
          point (e.g. “50.50” not “50.5”).  
        • No extra spaces anywhere on the lines.

   2.2  /home/user/capacity_planner/reports/peak_cpu_hours.log  
        • Five lines of plain text with a UNIX newline after each line.  
        • Required exact layout:
          HEADER:PEAK_CPU_HOURS
          1,<timestamp_1>,<percent_1>%
          2,<timestamp_2>,<percent_2>%
          3,<timestamp_3>,<percent_3>%
          FOOTER:END  
        • The three data lines must list, in **descending order**, the three
          timestamps with the highest cpu_percent values taken from the
          CSV file, along with their original percentage values (no rounding or
          re-formatting other than forcing exactly one digit after the decimal
          point when needed).  
        • Make sure to keep the line numbers “1,” “2,” and “3,” exactly as
          shown.

3.  Required calculations (all to be computed from usage_samples.csv)  
    • CPU_AVG        = arithmetic mean of the “cpu_percent” column  
    • MEM_AVG        = arithmetic mean of the “mem_used_mb” column  
    • DISK_MAX       = maximum of the “disk_used_gb” column  
    • NET_PEAK_IN    = maximum of the “net_in_kbps” column  
    • NET_PEAK_OUT   = maximum of the “net_out_kbps” column  
    For CPU_AVG and MEM_AVG the result must be rounded **half-up** to two
    decimal places.  For the three MAX / PEAK numbers, also print two decimal
    places even when the number is an integer (e.g. “123.00”).

4.  Concurrency requirement  
    The CPU/MEM averages and the DISK/NET maxima **must be derived in parallel
    processes**.  A simple way is to start two background subshells, redirect
    each one’s result to a temporary file, and wait for both to complete before
    generating the final log files.  Any parallelisation technique available
    in a POSIX shell is acceptable as long as the two groups of calculations
    run concurrently.

5.  Once all computations are finished, assemble the two log files in the
    “reports” directory exactly as specified above.

6.  Verification hint  
    When you are done, running
        cat /home/user/capacity_planner/reports/system_capacity_summary.log
    and
        cat /home/user/capacity_planner/reports/peak_cpu_hours.log
    should show the content in the precise format required.  The grader will
    compare your files byte-wise, including the trailing newline of each line.

7.  Important reminders  
    • Do not move, rename, or edit the original usage_samples.csv file.  
    • Keep the requested directory names and file names exactly as stated.  
    • Use only tools that are available in a typical POSIX shell environment
      (bash, awk, sort, cut, etc.).  
    • No sudo or root access is permitted or necessary.  
    • Paths in the final files must be relative-free (you have already been
      given the absolute paths).

This is an end-to-end, multi-step task.  Think through the calculations,
prepare your workspace, launch the parallel commands, wait for completion,
and finally write the two log files exactly in the specified formats.
truth:
Existing file before the agent starts
-------------------------------------
Path: /home/user/capacity_planner/input/usage_samples.csv  
Permissions: 0644 (user-writable)  
Content (5 lines, each terminated by \n):

timestamp,cpu_percent,mem_used_mb,disk_used_gb,net_in_kbps,net_out_kbps
2023-01-01 00:00,45.6,2300,120,300,250
2023-01-01 01:00,55.2,2400,121,350,270
2023-01-01 02:00,65.4,2500,123,400,310
2023-01-01 03:00,35.8,2200,119,280,200

Expected artefacts after successful completion
----------------------------------------------
1. /home/user/capacity_planner/reports/system_capacity_summary.log  
   Permissions: 0644  
   Exact 7-line content:

HEADER:CAPACITY_SUMMARY
CPU_AVG:50.50%
MEM_AVG:2350.00MB
DISK_MAX:123.00GB
NET_PEAK_IN:400.00Kbps
NET_PEAK_OUT:310.00Kbps
FOOTER:END

2. /home/user/capacity_planner/reports/peak_cpu_hours.log  
   Permissions: 0644  
   Exact 5-line content:

HEADER:PEAK_CPU_HOURS
1,2023-01-01 02:00,65.4%
2,2023-01-01 01:00,55.2%
3,2023-01-01 00:00,45.6%
FOOTER:END
